title: Push and non-fast-forward rejection

canonicalExplanation.summary: Push asks a remote to update selected references with local objects, subject to authentication, fast-forward, protection, and server policy.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A feature branch is pushed to a same-named remote branch after tests, then used to open review without updating protected main directly. Your goal is to push a feature branch and safely resolve a rejected update without force-overwriting others. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Branches, merges and conflicts; Pull policy; Short-lived feature branches. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Push asks a remote to update selected references with local objects, subject to authentication, fast-forward, protection, and server policy. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Force options, refspecs, tags, protected branches, hooks, credentials, and concurrent updates can reject or dangerously overwrite remote history. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Push uploads all local branches and files, and force push is the normal fix whenever the server rejects an update.

includes: Publish local refs only after reviewing destination, upstream and remote divergence.
Push a feature branch and safely resolve a rejected update without force-overwriting others.
excludes: Force options, refspecs, tags, protected branches, hooks, credentials, and concurrent updates can reject or dangerously overwrite remote history.

outcomes: Push a feature branch and safely resolve a rejected update without force-overwriting others.

examples.title: Push and non-fast-forward rejection: direct case
examples.situation: A feature branch is pushed to a same-named remote branch after tests, then used to open review without updating protected main directly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A feature branch is pushed to a same-named remote branch after tests, then used to open review without updating protected main directly.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Verify the source and destination refs, review outgoing commits, respect protection, and use force-with-lease only under an explicit history-rewrite policy.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Verify the source and destination refs, review outgoing commits, respect protection, and use force-with-lease only under an explicit history-rewrite policy.

examples.title: Push and non-fast-forward rejection: boundary case
examples.situation: A rejected non-fast-forward push triggers fetch and history inspection rather than an immediate force push.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A rejected non-fast-forward push triggers fetch and history inspection rather than an immediate force push.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Force options, refspecs, tags, protected branches, hooks, credentials, and concurrent updates can reject or dangerously overwrite remote history.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Push uploads all local branches and files, and force push is the normal fix whenever the server rejects an update.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Push a feature branch and safely resolve a rejected update without force-overwriting others.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Push uploads all local branches and files, and force push is the normal fix whenever the server rejects an update.
misconceptions.correction: Verify the source and destination refs, review outgoing commits, respect protection, and use force-with-lease only under an explicit history-rewrite policy.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Push uploads all local branches and files, and force push is the normal fix whenever the server rejects an update.

analogy.example: Two libraries exchanging catalog updates can model Push and non-fast-forward rejection: each keeps an independent collection and synchronizes selected records deliberately.
usefulFor: Separating local history, remote observations, download, integration, and publication
limitations: Git refspecs, authentication, ancestry checks, force updates, and object transfer have no exact library equivalent.
Force options, refspecs, tags, protected branches, hooks, credentials, and concurrent updates can reject or dangerously overwrite remote history.
